item.title: Edits
item.content:encoded: Fixed a typo and clarified some text in description paragraph 6.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Great work.
If I have to pick 5 skips to represent the spirit of SCP Foundation,this one is definitely one of them.

Just out of curiosity, will it kill 2698-A if we turn SCP-2698 off?
Please don`t let this cute high priest die :)
This one was made on a whim, and it somehow made it and exceeded the expectations I have of it when I first wrote it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Mystery and unknowability" are hardly registered trademarks of H.P. Lovecraft and the Cthulhu Mythos crew. If you're looking for things that commonly appear as story elements that our two general communities share, it's themes of forbidden knowledge and academia trying to come to terms with things that don't make sense. Again, though, those things are not sole property of Lovecraft and have been around a lot longer than either of us.

We could have a big long discussion about how much the site owes whoever in terms of influence. None of that really matters in the end, though. Articles now and in the future are beholden only to the readership, and if that means that things eventually (God willing) travel away from tired and predictable "throw some tentacles on it" Lovecraft service, then so be it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
the "known and understandable"

I think that's more with the current trend of articles (Series III). If we go by the older articles (Series I), they do adhere to a degree of mystery and unknowability. And they are where the site was founded upon.

Although that has shifted for a take on science fiction that would like explanations to phenomena, rather than just hollering at the unknowability of reality.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nah. I'd say that we run too far into the "known and understandable" to be anything more than Lovecraft-lite.

Lovecraft never made a mythos. That was Derleth's work, and Derleth managed to undercut much of what Lovecraft built. A mythos means some sort of ordering to the material, links between things that can be understood, even a little bit, and the understandable defaults to lovecraft-lite.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
To me it's lazy. "Lovecraft + X" repeated ad nauseam.

Shit, I even did it myself once. I tried to do "Lovecraft + Japanese pop music."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A sentiment I can perfectly agree with, but Lovecraftian tropes are the core and lifeblood of sites like this (if I daresay).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm pretty spent on "Lovecraftian" stuff.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have to Google who's Jerry Falwell 'cos of this.

Anyway, 2698-A does not have a "stance against homosexuality". It has a stance against pedophilia, with it regarding humans as larvae. It ties in with how humans relate to the Deep Ones in Lovecraft's works, like how humans would grow to be more Deep Ones-like in maturity.

I would say that how 2698-A regards human-octopus action is paralleled to infamous Catholic priest abuse cases, which it sees as a hurtful stereotype.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: For the sake of making up my mind (and I do not like an "either X or Y" scenario on papered documents), let's go with amnestics. And I am not implying that they will do that to GoI members in general or in specific; I perceive all things to run on a case-by-case basis.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I can see that, but I still think D-Class is overkill, and even potentially dangerous. Sure, they're totally inept and got very lucky when they completed an anomalous ritual, but they did complete that ritual. That's not the kind of thing you voluntarily put in close proximity to a Keter-class object.

That, and I'm opposed to the D-classing of non-violent GOI members (or probably even violent ones) on principle. By your own argument, they're basically just civilians with a weird hobby and good/bad luck. Plus, if you did that to the actual GOIs, that would set an incredibly bad precedent. You D-Class a captured GOC or MC&D member, and you start getting your agents back in pieces.

I'm no-voting on that basis. Tempted to downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I went with a direct reference, since I figured people will call me out on it if I omit it.

As for the CDO, I envision them as a joke GoI. 2698 was more of a lucky mistake, and they don't really have any real know-how to the paranormal.

+ show block
– hide block

Take this for reference:

Partial recitation of said incantation does not pose any health hazard to reciters. Pending approval for a full recital of the incantation.

It implies that their material may not be really anomalous, and that their plan worked due to some miscalculations along the way (which may be why reproductions of the ritual cannot work).

I won't say they would be researchers since their "anomalous skillset" is the result of accidents. They are more likely to be low-level personnel or skilled D-class.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yay Laundry Files! Initially it was a bit hard to swallow the direct reference, but it came together well. I also think that this rewrite strongly benefits from the additional story threads.

If I have one complaint, it's the final disposition of the CDO. They're a semi-dangerous GOI, sure, but faked deaths and conversion to D-Class isn't something you see with any other weird GOI. I've said it before, but D-Class implies a death sentence regardless of the author's headcanon. Often, I'd say that people capable of summoning an extradimensional entity should be recruited as researchers, but their motives leave something to be desired on that front. I don't see why amnesctization (or, worst-case, indefinite detention) wouldn't suffice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If I get to say the concept of this aloud, it'd probably suck balls, but I did enjoy the result. But what mae me upvote in this were the little details, and to point it out, I enjoyed the 4th footnote. I just love how it shows the Foundation's paranoia for everything that would smell like anomalous. It goes deeper than the Author probably intended, or maybe I just overthought about. Still, there are more nitpicks like that that I enjoyed, so I'm upvoting it.

item.title: Update Notice
item.content:encoded:
Highlighting the SCP's updated based on available feedback and my self-criticism of it. Changes include:

  • Changed the CDO member's dialogue such that it's more of him bragging his "achievement" to the agent.
  • Highlighted CDO as an university interest group, rather than an occult group.
  • Added a new footnote regarding the recitation of the incantation, which would (hopefully) open a new can of worms (so to speak).
  • Use a subtler means to put in eldritch abominations without using the actual phrase. While off from my intent, I guess it works with an audience familiar with it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Uh… if anything, it's more Lovecraftian than anti-hentai IMO. Although the base is still said "Lovecraftian anti-hentai thing", the execution of it pays homage to Lovecraftian tropes.

But yeah, some things tend not to be one's cup of tea. It's alright that way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
in-universe characters are just sort of flippantly tossing out the term "eldritch abomination" with a straight face.

I went with very blatant references since I've opted that this article is of a less serious extract. From the in-universe POV, it's more of the characters wanting to emulate what they see and know from fiction. Hence, they quoted it directly without care.

I don't buy for a second that someone who was a member of an occult group that witnessed real events of a paranormal nature and succeeded in summoning an extranormal entity is this blase about the whole affair.

Despite CDO being referred to as an occult group, it is only self-described and the Foundation went with it. They don't really do occult stuff beyond 2698, and are more of an interest group or school club. Their reaction to the whole affair would be more of awe and "Wow, that's cool". I don't really like the traditional mental trauma due to those creatures' very presence, resulting in a characterisation based on the CDO member bragging how he did it.

Your point on the characterisation will be noted though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh, not the Lovecraftian anti-hentai monster again. I just don't think this concept works, I'm sorry.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's really cool what you did with this concept. Also…

Controlled testing indicates that summoning techniques described in The Laundry Files are incapable of summoning extra-universal entities.

This is the best thing ever.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Mr. █████: Uh… you know, eldritch abominations, Lovecraftian monstrosities… those kind of things.

This line encapsulates nicely my problems with this article. I dislike greatly how lazy the site has become with Lovecraftian themes, to the point where in-universe characters are just sort of flippantly tossing out the term "eldritch abomination" with a straight face. Even if this particular influence weren't generally played out around here (which it most definitely is), this is an extremely clumsy implementation of that trope.

There's also the tone of this. I don't buy for a second that someone who was a member of an occult group that witnessed real events of a paranormal nature and succeeded in summoning an extranormal entity is this blase about the whole affair. That's not to say that he should be a stock shell-shocked trauma case that keeps muttering "the horror" over and over. But this, like so many other pieces, suffers from characterization that draws more from how people act on the chat than from actual human nature.

It's possible that I can get behind an entity whose primary motivation is censoring cephalopod-on-human interaction. That's a tad silly and very much the imposition of human norms on something that isn't human, but maybe that could work. But if you're playing this straight on the mainlist, then play it straight. This is way too goofy and detached from relateable human reactions to take seriously, for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Why are we allowing it unrestricted access to the internet?

There's a Faraday cage surrounding the chamber to deter such action, clearly stated in the first few lines of the procedures. It is currently contained and cannot access the Internet. I have not (in anywhere) stated that the skip is allowed Internet access, if that's your nitpick.

If anything, it is no more different from a normal computer with Internet access. The only real difference is that an otherworldly creature is the user and Internet security is nothing to it.

As proof, view all the revisions and you'll find this line present in all of them.

SCP-2698 is to be kept in a modified containment chamber at Site-15, lined with a Faraday cage.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
'SCP-2698-A is capable of accessing the Internet, and has unrestricted access to any online file. It is also able to bypass security measures such as passwords and firewalls without detection.'

Why are we allowing it unrestricted access to the internet? If this is an anomalous capabilty, this should be mentioned and treated as if the SCP is uncontained. Direct access to billoins of people is an ongoing containment breach, and should be treated as such.

I'd upvote other than that, but neutral for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh, that's interesting since the last interview was added in last minute. But yeah, the split is the first+second v.s. third.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Actually, it was the first and second ones which felt odd to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
…it's Cthulu as Jerry Falwell.

Well written, but feels somewhat derivative and flopping back and forth between silly/serious. Neutral vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh man, I was hoping this would make it to the main list. Bravo, MrWrong. Even better than the original draft I read! Just fantastic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Out of curiosity, is it mainly due to the last interview? Does it feel a bit off from the first, if I gauge you correctly?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's amusing to see an SCP censor the tentacles, too.

Precisely, I thought it was too funny of an image to bypass. I originally wanted to use Legend of the Overfiend or the Fisherman's Dream… but I'm not sure if the staff's okay with adult material (since I will censor tentacles and not the naked humans).

Anyway, I just used a normal picture and find the absurdity is better that way (PS. Should use a person entangled by a vine).

PS. If Image Staff reads this, please share what is the Staff's position towards using images of naked humans even if they are under CC0 or CC by attribution.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I remember the original SCP. Good job on the reimagining.

Also, tentacles in Japanese porn are commonly used because it is illegal to depict a penis penetrating anything sexually, even in animated form, so tentacles were a way to circumvent this without resorting to clumsy censoring. It's amusing to see an SCP censor the tentacles, too.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is quite possibly the easiest upvote i've given in a long time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like the ideas here, but the interviews just didn't click with me. I could go either way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
After some self-time, I think I will cut out "sentient" altogether and go with "organisms or automatons".

hope nobody educates it otherwise…

In an earlier version, I have Dr. Tarou note that it is a strategy to interact with 2698-A. I cut that out, so that readers would figure that out for themselves.

What I wonder is the Foundation's inability to isolate this thing.

The Faraday cage does the job. It accesses the Internet like any normal computer would. Even more ideally, the best way to disconnect it is to turn it off.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ahahaa oh man. I like the way those interview logs were written and paced. You get a +1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Perhaps Sapient? Sentience implies the ability to feel (which it does appear to have as well). Neither term applies exclusively to physicality, just to consciousness.

I like the idea of summoning a servant-of-the-elder-gods into a "fishtank". I get a bit of a chuckle of it believing a researcher to be an Elder God; hope nobody educates it otherwise…

What I wonder is the Foundation's inability to isolate this thing. Does it mystically connect to a nearby source? If it is already within a faraday cage yet still able to connect to the internet, what purpose is it serving? Ideally, we can contain this by just unplugging it from any outside communications.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I liked Dreamwalker Fae's, and I like this one too.

Plus one (1)

Vilz

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
the 'Lovecraftian horror' is the follower of humanity.

Not really, humans are deemed infants to 2698-A. It's just that it does not know that humans are speaking to it. As far as 2698-A is concerned, it is communing with the gods a la Moses on Mount Sinai. It would only feel scandalised if it knew the people behind the words.

First, sentient in the first footnote probably would not be the best term for cephalopods, given it usually brings thoughts to intelligent rather than animate.

I wanted to include the possibility of robotic tentacles or even vines… although I can understand your intent behind "animate".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Phenomenal concept work. It's amazing what can be done with a jokey premise, or even just making anything out of these weird concepts we as a species have created for ourselves, like tentacle porn. I also like that the 'Lovecraftian horror' is the follower of humanity. The whole thing melds so well into cohesive picture of what this is and why, without telling everything is something to look up to in writing. The few negatives (see below) barely touch the good. You have a hearty +1 from me!

the smallest of complaints
the smallest of boxes

If I had to comment on what could be improved, I'd say three things. First, sentient in the first footnote probably would not be the best term for cephalopods, given it usually brings thoughts to intelligent rather than animate. Second, the description says that SCP-2698-A is capable of accessing the internet, which should probably be altered to saying it can access networks connected to SCP-2698, including the internet. The third, that the forewords of the -A interviews should probably be two sentences like the other one, or separated with a semicolon or something.

item.title: Thanks, Attributions, Homage and Other Matters
item.content:encoded:
Firstly, allow me to thank some reviewers such as azzleflux, KholetteDrake, SoundChaser among others for them reading through and giving me guidance.

  • Picture 1 credited to Pixbay (https://pixabay.com/en/squid-fountain-ink-pen-silhouette-153604/) under CC0 Public Domain
  • Picture 2 credited to Wikipedia (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Callistoctopus_ornatus) under CC0 Public Domain

This SCP article is an unofficial "rewrite in spirit" (re: reimagining) of a previous version of SCP-2698 by DreamwalkerFae, who was in turn prompted by MrCobalt…

A "tentacle monster" that is sentient, capable of speech, and actually rather cultured. Wants/is trying to sue Japan for defamation.

That said, I failed to adhere to the challenge since tentacle erotica is not stated-owned and is not exclusively Japanese.

There are some homages to Lovecraftian genre as a whole.

+ show block
– hide block

Besides the obvious Laundry Files references, the "Dark Ocean" is based on Digimon… whereby the Dark Ocean is an alternate dimension inhabited by unknown creatures resembling the Deep Ones.

SCP-2698-A claims to be a high priest, a title held by Cthulhu. And humans being larvae echoes back to the Deep Ones.
